Note



“Dependency ratio, an important indicator of the amount of time, energy, and money the working age population must devote to non workers.”
— Folbre and Nelson (2000)





Measuring household time allocation

beneficial to understand how tasks and resources are allocated within a household.
The most common method is by utilizing time use surveys which usually collected by national bureaus or agencies
Miller and Sedai (2021) broaden the data source by using panel-time diaries to account the effect of time-varying variables



(Older) Household heads had to reduce time allocated for other than caregiving

For every additional hour of caregiving activities, study from Miller and Sedai (2021) shows that allocated time for work, leisure, home production, and personal care are reduced.
The panel time-use data shows that women have a greater time reduction on caring for self rather than men, thus inflicting health degradation.
We are also interested if the reduction of other life aspects because of non-spousal care also applies to younger household heads.



Treating PUDO trips as unpaid caregiving activities can be a first step to understand relationship between providing care and social welfare

Using Becker’s (1965) time allocation framework, we can see that the increasing care needs (increasing duration of caregiving) of a household member will effectively raises providers’ marginal benefit of giving care.
Caregiving activity includes all unpaid physical, medical, and school related assistance to others which often is a time intensive task (Miller and Sedai, 2021)



Potential of increased total economic production by putting nonmarket care-related activities into calculation

Folbre and Nelson (2000) argues the economy will seem to grow if we put value on non-market family-related or care-related activities and include them in calculation
Robert Eisner (1989) adds that the working hours of nonmarket activities accounts a significant amount of 40% to 60% from the total US output value
The Europe Union made effort to develop alternative income calculation by utilizing time-use data of activities
Calculation can also pushed further by taking account the value of household capital and raw materials (Ironmonger, 1996)



The Idea of Gross Economic Product by Ironmonger (1996)
\[GEP = GHP + GMP\]
Where:
\[GHP = W_H + K_H = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i \cdot w\right) + \left(K_{eq} + K_{hous}\right)\]
\[GMP = GDP - K_{hous}\]
\[GEP = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i \cdot w\right) + K_{eq} + K_{hous} + GDP - K_{hous} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i \cdot w\right) + K_{eq} + GDP\]
Variable descriptions:







Symbol
Description




\(GEP\)
Gross Economic Product — total value added by both household and market sectors


\(GHP\)
Gross Household Product — value added by unpaid household activities


\(GMP\)
Gross Market Product — GDP adjusted to remove double-counted owner-occupied housing


\(GDP\)
Conventional Gross Domestic Product as measured by the SNA


\(W_H\)
Value of unpaid household labor (~83% of GHP in Australia 1992)


\(h_i\)
Hours worked per week in household industry \(i\) (e.g. meal prep, child care, laundry)


\(w\)
Imputed wage rate per hour (AUD 14.25/hr in 1992)


\(n\)
Number of household industries (8 in the paper’s classification)


\(K_H\)
Total household capital contribution to GHP


\(K_{eq}\)
Imputed rental value of household equipment and vehicles


\(K_{hous}\)
Imputed rental value of owner-occupied housing (deducted from GDP to avoid double-counting)





The shift from household task to monetized service

Increasing trend of women participation in paid employment is believed by Folbre and Nelson (2000) as one aspect triggering the commodification of traditionally domestic tasks, including caring and homemaking activities
This is usually the case when doing more homemaking activities tends to lower parents earnings, thus indicating the increasing demand of care services due to longer paid working hours.
There is also another case when reallocate their non-market time and effort away from material production towards the personal and emotional actions



Factors affecting demand of paid care service (Folbre and Nelson, 2000)

Encouraging factors

expected freedom from doing bulk number of tedious homemaking activities
provide better quality of service, if:

caring activities seemed as matter of choice rather than necessity
costs being more widely and equitably distributed
get care services from those specially trained

enhance recipients’ sense of life control



Discouraging factors

Unable to replace personal care services that highly value the identity of provider and the continuity of the relationship
It is not guaranteed whether market competition will produce high quality care.
The market of care services may not be affordable equally across diverse groups.
Acute capitalization will make care services impersonal, thus produced at minimum cost and sold to the highest bidder




Factors affecting supply of paid care service (Morgan, 2005)

Encouraging factors

Responsibility of care is being left to private markets
Enormous supply of low-wage and low-skilled workers
Lack of unionization that could push wages higher and promote better working condition



Discouraging factors

Markets, even for services, are strictly controlled by government
Workforce rights are highly protected:

generous parental leave benefit
relatively high minimum wage
requirement of receiving specific vocational education

strong influence from worker unions




(Proposed) Research Question
This research proposal is currently structured around two interconnected phase/inquiries:

RQ 1

How to utilize PUDO trips as an alternative measurement of economic production



RQ 2

What factors causing PUDO trips as a care service hard to institutionalized?




(Proposed) Conceptual Framework

    A --> B["Capturing trips done for other's purpose"]

    B --> B1["Measure time and resource allocation"]

    B2 --> B2a

    subgraph C["Improved travel-diary surveys"]

        B2a["2nd layer questions"]
    end

    B2a --> B2b["Measuring the potential of marketization"]








(Proposed) Additional questions on Travel Diary Surveys

Layer 1: Capturing the trip

For whom is this trip done?
Is this trip your main activity? Or were you planning to go somewhere else before picking up / after dropping off?



Layer 2: Acknowledging the emotional relationship

Do you drive others because it is your responsibility?
Do you feel this activity will become a burden to others if you avoid or cancel?
Is this driving activity actively involved in building emotional relationships?
Is this activity reducing or included at the intended time?
For the passenger: “Do you feel losing control of your life when driven somewhere?”

title: Progress Report - 2026-06-08
text:
Three views of the same fortnight’s work. Pick whichever zoom level suits you.

Weekly Report — the long-form prose progress report, structured as a Horensou (報連相) status update to the supervisor. Best for reading top to bottom.
Slide Deck — the meeting-room presentation as a linear document, with action-title headings and the mermaid diagrams rendered inline.
Combined Detail — the integrated view where each section offers a Presentation tab (slide-style summary) and a Detailed tab (prose). Best for jumping into a single topic at two zoom levels.

The mermaid diagrams render as interactive SVG; pan and zoom should work in the browser.

Meeting metadata



Item
Content




Name
Rizqi Ramadhan


Meeting date
2026-06-08


Previous meeting date
2026-05-25









Notes on this document. The two weeks since the last meeting were used to (i) complete a 35-paper literature review on emotion in transport across three traditions — four anchor readings (Takai et al. 2025; Sheller & Urry 2006; Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao 2020; Rosenbloom 1987) plus 31 further papers from the emotion-phases bibliography of Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao 2020 — (ii) revise the narrative for Paper 1 in light of those readings, (iii) reformulate the research questions, and (iv) draft a four-module survey instrument that turns the revised construct into items grounded in cited literature.





1. What I want to discuss today (most important)

Whether the emotional-first reframing of Paper 1, in which the affective-relational structure is treated as constitutive of the demand rather than as a separate research question, is a defensible direction for the paper. The reframing keeps the triple-invisibility observation but argues that the three invisibilities have two roots (a modelling-convention root and an affective-relational root), not one. I would like Sensei’s view on whether this two-root framing strengthens or weakens the contribution.
Whether the research-question structure can be reduced from three parallel RQs to one primary RQ (capture of externally-derived demand including its affective-relational layer) and one secondary RQ (whether the affective-relational signal predicts behavioural variation beyond standard utility controls), with the resistance to marketization argument repositioned as a discussion-section implication rather than as a separate empirical question.
Whether descoping the DDCM / MDCEV time-allocation modelling into a follow-up paper (Paper 1.5) is acceptable for Year 1, with Paper 1 delivering the construct, the validated instrument, and the first descriptive evidence under the new instrument.



2. Current situation — progress since last meeting

What I did

Completed a 35-paper literature review on emotion in transport, organised across three traditions. Four anchor readings were the depth-read:

Takai, Chikaraishi & Fujiwara (2025) — relationship strength and social similarity as utility terms inside a discrete-choice framework, with significant effects on matching preferences and ridesharing use frequency. Methodological precedent for treating relational variables inside a utility framework.
Sheller & Urry (2006) — co-presence as a relational practice; the car interior as an inhabited space whose shared time has constitutive, not only instrumental, value. Theoretical warrant for the emotion as constituent of demand reading.
Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao (2020) — the instrumental versus affective-symbolic motive distinction; the three temporal phases of trip-related emotion (anticipated, in-trip, post-trip). Identifies the specific gap I claim: the affective-symbolic motive has been studied for the traveller’s own trips, not for trips made on behalf of another household member.
Rosenbloom (1987) — linked-to-work versus sole-purpose PUDO categories, with a disproportionate gendered share of escorting; the linked vs. sole distinction reads more defensibly as trip-chaining geometry than as intention, so Module B adds an intention layer rather than inheriting her framework.

The further 31 papers cited in the emotion-phases section of Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao (2020) were read at appropriate depth: nine in full, fifteen for instruments and findings, six for framing context, and one (Dittmar 1992) PDF-unavailable. They are grouped by the three emotion phases plus a conceptual bucket, and rendered as a paired descriptive + survey-design table per phase. The 35-paper review maps onto the four survey modules via a source table — every module’s items are grounded in specific cited literature, not invented. Three operative findings emerged from the review: (i) in-vehicle conversation lifts trip mood across three independent populations (Ettema et al. 2012 on Swedish PT commuters; Morris & Guerra 2015 on the US ATUS sample; Westman et al. 2017 on Swedish children); (ii) mainstream transport surveys already include “for others” categories but never cross them with the affect instrument layer — six papers establish that precedent (Mokhtarian & Salomon 2001; Ory & Mokhtarian 2009; Jakobsson-Bergstad et al. 2011; Delbosc & Currie 2011; McQuoid & Dijst 2012; Westman et al. 2017); (iii) the bridge from “for whose purpose” to affect-measured-with-STS is the methodological gap Paper 1 fills. McQuoid & Dijst (2012) is the single PUDO-visible empirical paper in the review (qualitative case studies of single mothers’ childcare drop/pickup paths) and is the only qualitative paper among the depth-reads — which formally signals that the qualitative tradition carries equal weight to the quantitative one in Paper 1’s literature review.
Revised the Paper 1 narrative to a two-root triple invisibility account: theoretical invisibility is rooted in the own-derived demand modelling convention (Becker 1965; Axhausen & Gärling 1992); methodological and economic invisibilities are rooted in the affective-relational structure that fuses provider and family roles. Documented in the revised narrative file.
Reformulated the research questions from three parallel RQs to one primary (capture-and-burden) + one secondary (behavioural-signal); the marketization-resistance question is repositioned as a discussion-section theoretical reading. Documented in 01_main/_research questions to guide literature review.md as a dated appended section.
Drafted the survey instrument as a coherent whole drawing on the literature review. Four modules added to a standard household travel diary: (A) trip purpose with a “for whom” question, an explicit “hard to tell” option for the initiating agent, and observable route accommodation as a behavioural proxy; (B) main-versus-secondary status with a slider that quantifies layered intention, plus displacement items; (C) closeness, contact, and obligation as three separate constructs — not the bundled “relationship strength” the discrete-choice tradition typically uses — collected once at intake, with positive-relations and preference-for-travel-with-others dispositional baselines; (D) anticipated, in-trip, and post-trip emotion across short Likert items, with an in-trip activity coding (conversation / quiet co-presence / task management / solitary) grounded in the strongest replicated empirical finding from the review. Each item carries its cited source. Estimated added burden: about 2–4 minutes per PUDO trip, within tolerance for standard household travel diaries.
Drafted three conceptual diagrams in mermaid for the report deck — overall framework; the three temporal phases of emotion (Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao 2020); DDCM versus MDCEV method-branching for the Paper 1.5 framing.
Produced two supporting deliverables for the next two weeks: a top-5 reading list for the week of 2026-06-08 (Friman et al. 2017 as the PLS-SEM template for Paper 1.5; Mesken et al. 2007 for the multi-method in-trip cadence; McQuoid & Dijst 2012 as the qualitative depth anchor; Tourangeau, Rips & Rasinski 2000 chapters 3 and 5 for cognitive-interview preparation; Folbre & Nelson 2000 for the care-work-and-money grounding); and a daily prose-writing practice routine grounded in the vault’s zettelkasten workflow, structured as a five-day weekday rhythm that converts reference excerpts into fleeting notes, fleeting notes into permanent notes, and permanent notes into prose paragraphs.



What I could not do / what is delayed

I have not yet obtained the DDCM entry paper from Sensei’s lab. The reference Sensei pointed to in class — Fosgerau, Frejinger & Karlström (2013) — is a recursive-logit route-choice paper, which is related to but not the same family of models as the time-allocation DDCM. I would like to confirm with Sensei whether the intent is for me to (a) use recursive logit directly as the model for PUDO sequencing or (b) use it as a methodological warm-up before selecting a time-allocation model.
I have not yet read Becker (1965) or Gronau (1977) in full. Both are foundational for the eventual valuation work; deferring them is acceptable for the current narrative-and-instrument phase but should not extend much further.
Cognitive interviewing of the survey items has not yet begun. I would like to discuss the recruitment plan and the prioritization of which items to test first.
Operational details of how the instrument will be paired with GPS tracking — if at all in Year 1 — remain open.




3. Definitions of terms







Term
Definition




PUDO trip
A trip whose purpose is to pick up or drop off another person — typically another household member — rather than to fulfil the driver’s own activity at the destination.


Externally-derived travel demand
A trip whose origin lies in another person’s activity-need rather than the traveller’s own, and whose value to the provider includes the affective-relational quality of the co-presence the trip produces. The first half extends the existing escort-trip category by identifying the initiating agent; the second half makes the relational dimension part of the construct itself rather than a discussion-section appendix.


Two-root triple invisibility
The framing in the revised narrative. Root 1 (modelling convention) produces theoretical invisibility: activity-based travel demand assumes demand is own-derived, leaving externally-derived demand under-specified. Root 2 (affective-relational structure) produces methodological and economic invisibility: providers experience the trip as relational care rather than as a service, blurring the for-whom boundary in surveys and making market substitutes a systematically wrong proxy for the trip’s value.


Anticipated, in-trip, and post-trip affect
The three temporal phases of trip-related emotion identified by Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao (2020). Anticipated affect is felt at the decision moment (“I would feel bad if I didn’t pick him up”). In-trip affect is felt during the journey (conversation, presence, stress). Post-trip affect is the evaluative emotion after the trip (“glad I did it”). The survey instrument measures each phase with separate items.


Felt closeness vs. contact intensity vs. felt obligation
The three relational constructs deliberately measured separately in the survey instrument. Takai et al. (2025) operationalize relationship strength as a bundle dominated by contact frequency. The instrument decomposes the bundle: closeness is dispositional emotional bond; contact intensity is observable frequency; felt obligation is the sense of responsibility for the other’s mobility. These constructs are correlated but not identical, and decoupling them empirically is part of the methodological contribution.


Construct, instrument, and behavioural signal
The three layers of the Paper 1 contribution. The construct is conceptual (externally-derived demand with affective motivation). The instrument is methodological (the survey modules). The behavioural signal is empirical (route detour, trip frequency, willingness-to-substitute as functions of the affective-relational items).





4. My own thoughts

On discussion item 1 — the emotional-first reframing with two-root triple invisibility

Current thought. The reframing strengthens the paper because it makes the measurement of emotion non-optional. In the previous narrative the relational claim risked being a rhetorical opening with no empirical instantiation; in the revised narrative the relational claim requires specific survey items (Module C closeness/obligation; Module D anticipated/in-trip/post-trip affect) and a behavioural signal (Module A detour). The two-root framing also resolves a subtle inconsistency in the earlier “RQ3 explains everything” alternative narrative — theoretical invisibility has a modelling-convention root that does not need emotion to explain, and is honest about that.
Reasoning. A reviewer who is asked to accept that emotion drives both survey-design failures and economic-account failures has to be convinced once. A reviewer who is told that the modelling convention explains the theoretical leg (a small, technical move) and the affective-relational structure explains the methodological and economic legs (a larger, evidenced move) has an easier time accepting both. Splitting the claim makes each half easier to defend.
Alternatives I considered. Keeping the original “single-root, triple-invisibility” framing because it is rhetorically tighter. The cost is that the theoretical-invisibility claim becomes harder to defend; the original framing implicitly asserts that emotion is the reason transport theory does not have a category for externally-derived demand, which is a stretch.



On discussion item 2 — the reduction from three parallel RQs to one primary plus one secondary

Current thought. The reduction reflects what Paper 1 can actually answer in Year 1. The construct-and-instrument question is answerable through design, pilot, and validation work. The behavioural-signal question is answerable through descriptive evidence and simple regression. The marketization-resistance question, as originally posed, requires either a stated-preference experiment offering market substitutes at varied prices or a longitudinal observation of resistance to a real market intervention — neither is feasible inside Paper 1, and forcing it in would weaken the paper.
Reasoning. The behavioural signal that Paper 1 collects (detour distance as a function of closeness; willingness-to-wait as a function of felt obligation) is itself the evidence for the marketization-resistance argument. The marketization argument is then the theoretical reading of that behavioural pattern, developed in the discussion using Folbre & Nelson (2000), Morgan (2005), and the mobilities literature. This is a more honest division of labour between empirical and theoretical claims than treating marketization-resistance as a third stand-alone RQ.
Alternatives I considered. Keeping three RQs and accepting that RQ3 would be answered only theoretically. The cost is that the paper appears to make three empirical claims while in fact only two have empirical content, which invites the question of why the third is included at all.



On discussion item 3 — descoping DDCM / MDCEV to Paper 1.5

Current thought. Paper 1 should deliver the construct, the validated instrument, and the descriptive evidence under the new instrument. Time-allocation modelling using DDCM or MDCEV requires the data that Paper 1’s instrument will eventually collect; it is structurally a follow-up paper, not a co-equal element of Paper 1. Compressing both into one paper risks delivering neither in Year 1.
Reasoning. The DDCM / MDCEV choice is itself non-trivial — DDCM captures sequencing and displacement, MDCEV captures portfolio share, and they answer different questions. Making that choice well requires the descriptive picture that Paper 1’s instrument provides. Reversing the order — choose model first, then design instrument — is the failure mode flagged in the May 22 RQ2 critique.
Alternatives I considered. Folding a small MDCEV or DDCM application into Paper 1 as a secondary contribution. The risk is that it crowds the methods section, makes the paper hard to position (instrument paper or model paper?), and produces preliminary numbers that the reader may treat as central. Keeping the modelling work for Paper 1.5 keeps Paper 1’s contribution focused.




5. Where I am stuck / what I cannot decide on my own

The right empirical scope for the affective-relational module. The instrument currently has Module C (dispositional, intake) and Module D (per-trip, sampled). I am uncertain whether sampling per-trip affect on, say, every fourth trip is statistically sufficient to support the behavioural-signal claim in RQ2, or whether full administration on every PUDO trip is required at the cost of burden.
Sample design and recruitment frame. The deployment platform is solved: the instrument will be embedded inside Reem’s travel-diary app, on which we have already aligned, which removes the infrastructure-build risk. What remains open is the recruitment frame for the cognitive interview and pilot stages — Hiroshima University community, recruited Hiroshima household sample, or a small staged convenience pilot — and I would like Sensei’s guidance on which path to pursue through the Hiroshima University IRB (the first clearance gate; whether subsequent gates apply is not yet confirmed).
The Fosgerau–Frejinger–Karlström (2013) routing question. I cannot resolve, without Sensei, whether that paper is meant to be the actual model for PUDO sequencing or a methodological warm-up before selecting a time-allocation DDCM. The two intents imply different reading plans.
Whether the marketization-resistance argument carries enough weight as a discussion implication. Even after the repositioning, I want to make sure the discussion has enough substance — and enough policy relevance — to justify the introduction’s promise that the paper will speak to why PUDO stays personal.



6. Things I want to confirm

Is the two-root triple-invisibility framing acceptable, or does Sensei prefer the original single-root framing for rhetorical reasons?
Is the reduction from three parallel RQs to one primary + one secondary RQ acceptable, with the marketization-resistance argument repositioned as a discussion implication?
Is descoping the DDCM / MDCEV modelling into a follow-up Paper 1.5 acceptable, or does Sensei want a small model section inside Paper 1?
Confirming Fosgerau, Frejinger & Karlström (2013) as background reading for the broader dynamic-discrete-choice toolkit, with the specific time-allocation entry paper to be identified by Sensei from his lab’s work — is that the right reading order?
For the cognitive interviewing of the survey instrument, would Sensei prefer recruitment from (a) Hirodai community, (b) a recruited Hiroshima household sample, or (c) a small convenience pilot before any formal sampling?



7. Computational results / figures
No quantitative results were produced this fortnight. The conceptual diagrams (overall framework; three temporal phases of emotion; method-branching for time-allocation modelling) are rendered in the Slide Deck and Combined Detail tabs of this page; rendered versions are also brought to the meeting on slides.


8. Supplementary materials

Revised narrative — emotional-first, two-root triple invisibility — draft narrative document.
Reformulated research questions — primary + secondary RQ structure (also appended in 01_main/_research questions to guide literature review.md).
Survey instrument — full question bank for Modules A–D with literature sources and burden estimates per item.
Literature review matrix — 35 papers on emotion in transport, grouped by emotion phase, with PUDO-precedent flags and a source-table mapping each module to the cited literature behind it.
Conceptual framework diagrams — overall framework; Transport and Emotion (Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao, 2020); DDCM versus MDCEV branching.
Reference notes for the four anchor readings.
Top-5 reading list for the week of 2026-06-08 — Friman et al. 2017; Mesken et al. 2007; McQuoid & Dijst 2012; Tourangeau et al. 2000 chapters 3 and 5; Folbre & Nelson 2000.
Daily prose-writing practice routine — a five-day weekday rhythm grounded in the vault’s zettelkasten workflow, intended as ongoing practice rather than a one-off deliverable.










The slide deck flattened into a linear document, with action-title headings and mermaid diagrams rendered inline. Section headers correspond to the six top-level sections of the original presentation.




1 · Frame — where I was, and what shifted

Two weeks ago: three parallel RQs, one rhetorical frame, no new readings to anchor either
The 25 May report committed to a triple-invisibility framing for Paper 1 — PUDO trips invisible in theory, in surveys, and in economic accounts — and proposed three parallel research questions: a survey-design RQ, an economic-valuation RQ, and a marketization-resistance RQ. None had been tested against new empirical or theoretical reading.




25 May state




Narrative
Single-root triple invisibility; four-act arc


RQs
Three parallel (survey / valuation / resistance)


New readings
None this cycle


Survey
No items drafted


Modelling

3 · Narrative revision — two roots, one motivating observation, one construct pair

The original “single-root” framing implied emotion explained all three legs — but theoretical invisibility has a different cause








Invisibility leg
Original claim (25 May)
Problem




Theoretical
Activity-based theory has no category for externally-derived demand
True, but the cause is a modelling convention (utility maximization of own activity), not emotion


Methodological
Surveys ask with whom, not for whom
The cause genuinely is the affective-relational fusion of provider and family roles


Economic
Unpaid care labour excluded from production accounts
The cause is also affective-relational — market substitutes systematically misprice the trip



If the single-root framing tries to make emotion explain the theoretical leg too, a reviewer can knock the whole frame down by pointing out that escort trips are already a recognised category — just an under-specified one. Splitting the causes keeps the punch of the triple-invisibility framing while letting each leg stand on its own.

RQ1 — What survey design captures externally-derived demand including its affective-relational motivation, at acceptable burden?

RQ1a (construct definition): What is the precise condition that makes a trip count as externally-derived, and how does it extend the escort-trip category?
RQ1b (turning the construct into items): Which items capture (i) the initiating agent, (ii) main vs. secondary status, (iii) the affective-relational motivation, and (iv) closeness, contact, and obligation as separable constructs?
RQ1c (burden and validity): Do the new items preserve completion rates, and do respondents interpret them as intended?
RQ1d (visibility delta): Under the new instrument, what share of household trips appear as PUDO that standard diaries miss?



RQ2 — Does the affective-relational signal predict provider behaviour beyond standard utility controls?

RQ2a (behavioural signal): Does anticipated emotion / felt closeness predict observable variation — route detour distance, willingness-to-wait, trip frequency — once standard utility variables are controlled?
RQ2b (decomposition): Can the affective-relational variance be decomposed into closeness, anticipated emotion, and post-trip satisfaction? Which carries most predictive weight?
RQ2c (cross-context generalization): Does the relational-utility finding documented across the discrete-choice, qualitative escort-trip, and affective-symbolic-motive traditions generalize to a household-PUDO observational context inside one unified instrument?




5 · Survey instrument — four modules, ~2–4 minutes extra per PUDO trip

A wider review of 35 papers maps cleanly onto the four survey modules
After the four anchor readings, a wider review of the 31 emotion-and-travel papers cited in Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao (2020) was completed this week. The combined 35-paper set maps onto the four survey modules as shown below — every module’s items are grounded in specific cited sources, not invented. The single most replicated finding from the wider review is that in-vehicle conversation lifts trip mood: three independent studies converge on it — Ettema et al. (2012) on Swedish public-transport commuters, Morris & Guerra (2015) on a large US national sample, and Westman et al. (2017) on Swedish children. That finding directly motivates Module D’s in-trip activity coding.

Two design moves keep the burden down: the closeness/contact/obligation items are collected once at intake instead of every trip, and the affect items are asked on only a sample of trips instead of every one. Standard household travel diaries run 15–30 minutes per day; this instrument adds about 10 minutes per day in the worst case (four PUDO trips, two sampled for affect).


Module A — separates for whom from with whom, and uses observable route accommodation as a behavioural proxy
Module A separates for whom the trip is made from with whom the person travels — a distinction no standard household travel diary asks. The new activity code “To take or pick up someone in my household” attaches to A1, so the diary stays backward-compatible. A2’s “hard to tell” option for whose activity-need motivated the trip is itself a measurement: the share of respondents who choose it is a direct test of the fused-role idea (provider role and family role experienced as one). A5–A7 add observable route accommodation as a behavioural proxy for relational utility — did the provider choose a longer route, how much longer, and why. These items pair a self-reported feeling in Module D with an observable behaviour, so the affective claim is not left hollow.

Module B — accepts that intention is layered, and asks respondents to quantify the split
The standard primary/secondary distinction collapses on PUDO trips piggy-backed onto another trip. Module B accepts that: B1 offers “some of both”; B2 asks respondents to quantify the split with a slider (0 = entirely my destination; 100 = entirely their need); B3–B4 capture displacement. The slider in B2 is the methodological risk; cognitive interviewing will test whether respondents can give a stable answer.

Module C — splits bundled relationship-strength into three constructs the discrete-choice tradition hides
The discrete-choice tradition typically bundles relational variables into a single “relationship strength” dominated by contact frequency. Module C deliberately decouples felt closeness (C2), contact intensity (C3), and felt obligation (C4). These three are correlated but not identical — close family with low contact frequency; high-contact colleagues with low obligation. Measuring them separately lets the analysis test the bundle, rather than reproduce it. C6 and C7 add dispositional baselines for partialling-out. Module C is collected once at intake.

Module D — measures emotion at three points in the trip’s life
Module D measures emotion at anticipated, in-trip, and post-trip points, following Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao’s three-phase framework. Short items only; sampled administration. The anticipated–experienced gap (D1 vs. D5) is a publishable finding in itself. D7’s in-trip activity coding is grounded in the strongest replicated finding from the wider review: in-vehicle conversation lifts trip mood across three independent populations.
Anticipated: D1 (4 affect items), D2 (felt obligation), D8 (anticipated multi-select) — sources: Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao 2020; PANAS; Folbre & Nelson 2000; McQuoid & Dijst 2012; Gärling 2019.

The literature review is one coherent activity covering 35 papers across three traditions. Four readings were the depth-read:

Takai, Chikaraishi & Fujiwara (2025) — relationship strength and social similarity as utility terms inside a discrete-choice framework. Methodological precedent for treating relational variables inside a utility framework.
Sheller & Urry (2006) — co-presence as relational practice; the car interior as an inhabited space. Theoretical warrant for the emotion as constituent of demand reading.
Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao (2020) — instrumental versus affective-symbolic motive; three temporal phases of trip-related emotion. Identifies the specific gap I claim.
Rosenbloom (1987) — linked-to-work vs. sole-purpose PUDO categories; disproportionate gendered share of escorting. Module B adds an intention layer rather than inheriting her framework.

The further 31 papers cited in the emotion-phases section of Souche-Le Corvec & Zhao (2020) were read at appropriate depth: nine in full, fifteen for instruments and findings, six for framing context, and one (Dittmar 1992) PDF-unavailable. They are grouped by the three emotion phases plus a conceptual bucket, and rendered as a paired descriptive + survey-design table per phase.
Three operative findings emerged:

In-vehicle conversation lifts trip mood across three independent populations (Ettema et al. 2012; Morris & Guerra 2015; Westman et al. 2017). Three countries, three age groups, three methods, one finding that does not go away.
Mainstream transport surveys already include “for others” categories but never cross them with the affect instrument layer. Six papers establish the precedent: Mokhtarian & Salomon 2001; Ory & Mokhtarian 2009; Jakobsson-Bergstad et al. 2011; Delbosc & Currie 2011; McQuoid & Dijst 2012; Westman et al. 2017.
The bridge from “for whose purpose” to affect-measured-with-STS is the methodological gap Paper 1 fills. Both layers individually well-established; the cross has not yet been made.

McQuoid & Dijst (2012) is the only PUDO-visible empirical paper in the review and is the only qualitative paper among the depth-reads — formally signalling that the qualitative tradition carries equal weight to the quantitative one in Paper 1’s literature review.

Current thought. The reframing strengthens the paper because it makes the measurement of emotion non-optional. The relational claim requires specific survey items (Module C closeness/obligation; Module D anticipated/in-trip/post-trip affect) and a behavioural signal (Module A detour). The two-root framing also resolves a subtle inconsistency in the earlier “RQ3 explains everything” alternative — theoretical invisibility has a modelling-convention root that does not need emotion to explain.
Reasoning. A reviewer asked to accept that emotion drives both survey-design failures and economic-account failures has to be convinced once. A reviewer told that the modelling convention explains the theoretical leg (a small, technical move) and the affective-relational structure explains the methodological and economic legs (a larger, evidenced move) has an easier time accepting both.
Alternatives considered. Keeping the original single-root framing because it is rhetorically tighter. The cost is that the theoretical-invisibility claim becomes harder to defend.





3 · Research questions — one primary + one secondary

PresentationDetailed



Reducing to one primary + one secondary RQ clarifies what Paper 1 can answer in Year 1










RQ1
RQ2
RQ3

Capture-focused survey design
DDCM or MDCEV for time allocation
Why PUDO resists marketization


New (v2)

Repositioned. Theoretical reading in the discussion


Modelling sits in
n/a

RQ1 — What survey design captures externally-derived demand including its affective-relational motivation, at acceptable burden?

RQ1a: What is the precise condition that makes a trip count as externally-derived?
RQ1b: Which items capture the initiating agent, main vs. secondary status, the affective-relational motivation, and closeness/contact/obligation as separable constructs?
RQ1c: Do the new items preserve completion rates, and do respondents interpret them as intended?
RQ1d: Under the new instrument, what share of household trips appear as PUDO that standard diaries miss?



RQ2 — Does the affective-relational signal predict provider behaviour beyond standard utility controls?

RQ2a: Does anticipated emotion / felt closeness predict route detour distance, willingness-to-wait, trip frequency, once standard utility variables are controlled?
RQ2b: Can the affective-relational variance be decomposed into closeness, anticipated emotion, and post-trip satisfaction?
RQ2c: Does the relational-utility finding documented across three traditions generalize to a household-PUDO context inside one unified instrument?




Current thought. The reduction reflects what Paper 1 can actually answer in Year 1. The construct-and-instrument question is answerable through design, pilot, and validation. The behavioural-signal question is answerable through descriptive evidence and simple regression. The marketization-resistance question requires either a stated-preference experiment or a longitudinal observation — neither feasible inside Paper 1.
Reasoning. The behavioural signal that Paper 1 collects (detour distance as a function of closeness; willingness-to-wait as a function of felt obligation) is itself the evidence for the marketization-resistance argument. The marketization argument becomes the theoretical reading of that behavioural pattern, developed in the discussion using Folbre & Nelson (2000), Morgan (2005), and the mobilities literature.
Alternatives considered. Keeping three RQs and accepting that RQ3 would be answered only theoretically. The cost is that the paper appears to make three empirical claims while in fact only two have empirical content.





4 · Survey instrument — four modules grounded in cited literature

OverviewModule AModule BModule CModule DImplementation path



Four modules add ~2–4 minutes per PUDO trip on top of a standard diary

Two design moves keep the burden down: the closeness/contact/obligation items are collected once at intake instead of every trip, and the affect items are asked on only a sample of trips. Standard diaries run 15–30 minutes per day; this instrument adds about 10 minutes per day in the worst case.




Module A — separates for whom from with whom, and uses observable route accommodation as a behavioural proxy
Module A separates for whom the trip is made from with whom the person travels — a distinction no standard household travel diary asks. The new activity code “To take or pick up someone in my household” attaches to A1. A2’s “hard to tell” option for whose activity-need motivated the trip is itself a measurement: the share of respondents who choose it is a direct test of the fused-role idea. A5–A7 add observable route accommodation as a behavioural proxy for relational utility.

title: Short-term Traffic Prediction Using Statistical Time Series Analysis. A Study Case of National Route 31, Hiroshima, Japan
text: Road traffic possesses a dynamic and anticipatory nature. Previous medium-long interval macro traffic models that require complex data are becoming irrelevant for dealing with the stochastic nature of traffic. Thus, short-term prediction approach come as a solution to model the dynamic and continuity of traffic. This research is structured to formulate short-term traffic flow prediction model alternatives, in which the best-performing model will be used to predict traffic flow at a downstream point on the National Route 31 arterial road, Kure City, Hiroshima, Japan. The models will utilize statistical time series analysis approach, consisted of ARIMA method and Dynamic Regression method. The modeling process resulted in 2 alternative ARIMA models and 10 alternative Dynamic Regression models.

title: Evaluating stakeholder structure and private actor involvement on the distribution of relief goods considering a partly centralised decision
Evaluating stakeholder structure and private actor involvement on the distribution of relief goods considering a partly centralised decision
Evaluating stakeholder structure and private actor involvement on the distribution of relief goods considering a partly centralised decision
text:
Abstract
Relief distribution involves numerous stakeholders with various roles and different structures regarding stakeholders’ coordination. This paper proposes a model to evaluate various stakeholder structures for distributing relief goods. Unlike previous research, this model evaluates the coordination structure, assuming no single centralised decision-maker exists. The proposed model is developed based on the decomposition-based framework, assuming a partly centralised decision. The proposed model also integrates road network restoration into the model by capturing the disrupted road network. The role of the private sector is elaborated by exploring the impact of private business involvement in food-related distribution and road network restoration. The proposed model is applied to an actual case, namely the Palu earthquake and tsunami in 2018. We found that the multifocal structure performs better in distributing relief goods. Furthermore, the involvement of the private sector succeeds in minimising the unsatisfied demand.

title: Progress Report — 2026-05-25
text:
Progress ReportAppendix - Research DocumentationFeedback and Action items



Notes on preparing this document

This week’s progress was largely conceptual and exploratory. The session with the AI copilot focused on stress-testing the three proposed research questions for Paper 1, drafting two competing narrative arcs, and assembling a structured reading list. No new calculations, equations, or figures were produced — the corresponding sections are therefore omitted.
Terms introduced this week (notably externally-derived demand) are defined explicitly in Section 3.
Full supporting material — proposed narrative, alternative narrative, and the consolidated literature list — is attached as appendices rather than embedded inline, to keep the main body of this document discussable.




1. What I Want to Discuss Today (Most Important)

Whether the triple-invisibility framing of Paper 1 (invisible in transport theory, in surveys, and in economic accounts) is a defensible spine for the paper, or whether it overstates the conceptual gap.
Whether the alternative narrative — leading with the relational/emotional dimension (current RQ3) as the motivating insight, rather than placing it as the third research question — is a better organization for the paper. This is a structural decision I cannot make alone.
Whether to commit early to DDCM (Sensei’s direction) over MDCEV (Reem’s suggestion), or to defer the choice until I have written a side-by-side comparison note grounded in Becker (1965) and Gronau (1977). I would also like guidance on whether Sensei has a specific lab paper he wants me to start from for DDCM.



2. Current Situation (Progress Since Last Meeting)

What I did

Initiated a structured AI-assisted brainstorming session to surface weaknesses in the three proposed research questions for Paper 1 before further reading.
Produced critical reviews — strengths, weaknesses, mentor questions, and reading priorities — for each of the three RQs: survey design (RQ1), economic valuation (RQ2), and resistance to marketization (RQ3).
Drafted two competing narrative arcs for Paper 1: a proposed four-act narrative built around the triple-invisibility framing, and an alternative narrative that reorders the argument so that the relational/emotional observation becomes the motivating insight rather than the third finding. Both are fleeting notes, not draft sections.
Assembled three clustered literature lists for the three RQs (survey design and escort trips; economic valuation and time allocation; emotional and relational dimensions). Each list is organized into clusters and annotated with priority and confidence levels.



What I could not do / what is delayed

I have not yet obtained or read Takai-san’s paper. It is the most important single source for both RQ1 (provider-beneficiary capture) and RQ3 (closeness as a utility term). I need Sensei’s help to identify the correct citation.
I have not yet read Becker (1965) in full, nor Gronau (1977). Both are foundational for RQ2 and I have so far been working from secondary summaries.
I have not started reading DDCM or MDCEV references. I would like to begin with the specific DDCM paper Sensei recommends rather than the broader continuous-time scheduling literature.
I have not begun operationalizing the emotional/relational concepts (closeness, obligation, responsibility) into draft survey items. (I marked this as a red flag: a concept I cannot turn into even a draft survey question is a concept I do not yet understand well enough.)
No mathematical work on the economic output calculation has begun. The valuation method (replacement cost vs. opportunity cost vs. utility-based VoT) is still unresolved.




3. Definitions of Terms







Term
Definition




PUDO trip
A trip whose purpose is to pick up or drop off another person — typically another household member — rather than to fulfill the driver’s own activity at the destination.


Externally-derived demand
A coined working term for trips whose origin lies in another person’s activity need rather than the traveler’s own. Standard activity-based travel theory assumes demand is internally derived (the traveler’s own activity initiates the trip); PUDO trips break that assumption. To be checked against existing literature before being treated as an original construct.


Escort trip
An existing category in activity-based travel surveys and trip chaining literature. Classifies the driver’s trip purpose (“escorting another person”) but does not identify whose need motivates the trip nor the relational or economic stakes. The proposed contribution is to extend, not replace, this category.


Provider–beneficiary distinction
The distinction between the household member performing the PUDO trip (provider) and the household member whose activity need is served (beneficiary). Current travel surveys capture with whom one travels, but not for whom the trip is done.


Main vs. secondary activity
A trip done as the principal motivation of an outing versus one piggy-backed onto another trip (e.g., dropping a child off while commuting). The distinction matters for valuation and is already known as an unsolved problem in time-use research (Miller & Sedai 2021).


Triple invisibility
The framing in the proposed narrative: PUDO trips are simultaneously (i) misclassified in transport theory, (ii) uncaptured in travel surveys, and (iii) excluded from economic accounts.


Relational resistance to marketization
The argument that PUDO trips are not commodified at scale because their value is partly constituted by the relationship between driver and passenger — a value a market substitute cannot reproduce.





4. My Own Thoughts

On discussion item 1 — the triple-invisibility framing

Current thought: I find the framing useful as a way to connect the three threads of Paper 1 into a single argument, but I am not yet sure each “invisibility” is equally well-grounded. The survey invisibility is clearly real (Miller & Sedai 2021 admit it openly). The economic-account invisibility is also clearly real (Ironmonger 1996; Folbre & Nelson 2000). The “invisibility in transport theory” claim is the one I am least sure about — escort trips are a recognized category, even if they are conceptually thin. I worry that claiming PUDO is invisible in transport theory could be overstating the gap.
Reasoning: The strongest version of the contribution is probably “extend an underspecified category” rather than “name an entirely missing category”. The weaker claim is easier to defend and probably closer to the truth.
Alternatives I considered: Reframing as a measurement gap rather than a theoretical gap; or keeping the triple framing but qualifying the third leg (“under-theorized” rather than “invisible”).



On discussion item 2 — proposed vs. alternative narrative

Current thought: The alternative narrative is conceptually more honest. If the relational dimension is what makes RQ1 and RQ2 hard at all, then it is the motivating insight of the paper, not the closing reflection. Leading with it would also make the introduction sharper and clarify why the survey instrument and the economic valuation matter.
Reasoning: The argument in the alternative narrative is that if PUDO were purely transactional, the survey question would be trivial and the replacement cost would be the correct valuation. Both questions are non-trivial because of the relational structure. That asymmetry suggests the relational claim is foundational rather than third.
Alternatives I considered: Keeping the proposed four-act structure but moving the emotional/relational claim into the introduction as a hook, while still treating it as RQ3 in the substantive analysis. This is a middle path. I am not yet sure whether it weakens the structural logic of the alternative narrative or sensibly compromises with audience expectations.



On discussion item 3 — DDCM vs. MDCEV

Current thought: I should not pick a model before I can articulate the research question precisely. The two models answer different questions: MDCEV is about cross-sectional allocation across activities (what share of the time portfolio is PUDO?); DDCM is about sequence and timing within a day (when does PUDO occur and how does it displace surrounding activities?). The choice has to follow the question, not the other way around.
Reasoning: If the question is how much time is allocated to PUDO and what is that worth, MDCEV is the closer fit. If the question is how does providing PUDO restructure the rest of the day, DDCM is the closer fit. Both are legitimate research questions, but they are different papers.
Alternatives I considered: A staged approach — start with MDCEV to characterize the time allocation cross-sectionally, then move to DDCM to capture the sequencing effects in a second paper. This is more honest about scope, but I am not yet sure whether it is realistic within Year 1.




5. Where I Am Stuck / What I Cannot Decide on My Own

Narrative structure decision (proposed vs. alternative). This is a judgment call about how to present the paper’s logic, and I do not yet have enough literature reading or supervisor feedback to make it confidently. The decision affects which sections receive the most space and which audience the paper targets.
Whether externally-derived demand is a defensible coined term or an existing concept under a different name. I do not yet know whether the activity-based modeling literature already has a term for “trip whose initiating agent is the passenger, not the driver”. Until I check, I am uncomfortable building the paper around the new term.
The boundary of “for others”. A parent driving a child to school on the way to work is serving both the child’s need and their own schedule. Where to draw the boundary affects every downstream calculation. I cannot write a precise two-sentence definition yet.
Value of Time approach. Choosing between replacement cost, opportunity cost, and utility-based VoT is not just a methodological preference — each implies a different conceptual claim about what is being valued. I am stuck partly because I have not yet read Becker, Gronau, and Jara-Díaz carefully enough to choose with confidence.



6. Things I Want to Confirm

Is there a specific Takai-san paper Sensei would like me to start with for the closeness-detour finding?
Is there a specific DDCM paper from Sensei’s lab that I should use as my entry point, rather than reading the broader continuous-time scheduling literature?
Is the audience for Paper 1 best understood as transport economists, care economists, or transport-policy practitioners? The narrative structure decision depends on this.
Is “improving the survey instrument” the primary methodological contribution of Paper 1, or is the economic valuation the primary contribution, with the survey as a methodological enabler? The framing changes the target journal.



7. Computational Results / Figures (if any)
No calculations, figures, or equations were produced this week. This section is intentionally left empty.




Appendix A — Proposed Narrative: PUDO as Triple Invisibility

This is a thinking tool, not a draft section.

Household PUDO trips are simultaneously invisible in transport theory (misclassified as self-motivated travel), invisible in surveys (no instrument captures for whom a trip is done), and invisible in economic accounts (unpaid care labor excluded from production calculations) — and grounding them as a distinct theoretical construct is the first step to making all three visible.

The four-act arc
Act 1 — The triple invisibility. PUDO trips are a significant share of household mobility yet are invisible at three distinct levels. Transport theory assumes every trip is derived from the traveler’s own activity need; PUDO trips break this assumption (externally-derived demand). Travel surveys ask with whom one travels but not for whom. Economic accounts exclude unpaid household labor altogether. The triple invisibility reflects a systematic failure across transport modeling and economic accounting to recognize care-embedded activity as either theoretically meaningful demand or economically productive output. Act 1 leaves the reader asking: why has nobody defined this as a distinct category before?
Act 2 — Making PUDO visible: a new construct and a survey that measures it. The first intervention is conceptual, with a methodological output. Before a new survey question can be designed, externally-derived travel demand must be defined as a construct: trips whose origin lies in another person’s activity need rather than the traveler’s own. The contribution is to extend and theoretically ground the existing but underspecified “escort trip” category — specifying the provider–beneficiary structure and designing survey items that capture whose demand is being served, including the main vs. secondary activity distinction. The Takai-san finding (closeness affecting route choice utility) becomes confirmatory: beneficiary identity is already shaping behavior in the data; the survey innovation surfaces what is already latent.
Act 3 — Valuing PUDO: economic output calculation. With improved survey data, the economic value of PUDO trips can be estimated through two complementary questions: a time-allocation question (how does providing PUDO affect a provider’s time across all activities? — DDCM for timing/sequencing, MDCEV for total time portfolio) and a valuation question (given the time cost, what is the equivalent economic value? — opportunity cost, replacement cost, or utility-based VoT, with the choice justified rather than assumed). Grounding sources include Becker (1965), Gronau (1977), and the SNA satellite account literature.
Act 4 — Why it stays personal: the resistance to marketization. The economic calculation prompts a question: if PUDO is this costly, why don’t households outsource it? Drawing on Folbre & Nelson (2000), Morgan (2005), and care ethics literature, Act 4 argues that PUDO trips are embedded in relationships of closeness, trust, and obligation that cannot be reproduced through market transactions. This has direct transport-policy implications: ride-hailing programs struggle to replace family PUDO because the relational value of the trip cannot be substituted. The survey instrument designed in Act 2 can capture these dimensions through emotional/relational items alongside travel behavior.
Conclusion. By making PUDO visible and giving it economic value, the paper can improve transport demand modeling (PUDO is systematically underestimated), revise household economic production accounts (PUDO falls outside any existing satellite framework), and inform care and transport policy (households bear invisible care costs that markets cannot or should not fully absorb). The paper does not argue PUDO should be commodified — it argues it should be counted.


How the three RQs connect

Open questions on the proposed narrative

Is Act 4 a research question or a discussion section? If the emotional/relational claim cannot be tested empirically within the paper, it may belong in the introduction as motivation rather than in the results.
Is the paper making a Japan-specific claim or a universal one? Morgan’s varieties-of-capitalism frame implies Japan matters but this is not yet stated.
Who is the primary audience — transport economists, care economists, or public health economists? Different audiences expect different acts to receive the most space.
Is the survey instrument the primary output, or is the economic valuation the primary output? The emphasis affects which journal the paper targets.
Is externally-derived demand the right term? It must be checked against the activity-based modeling literature before being treated as original.





Appendix B — Alternative Narrative: Leading with the Relational Structure

A counter-organization of the same material as Appendix A. The two narratives are not yet reconciled.


Starting point: why are RQ1 and RQ2 hard at all?
If PUDO trips were purely functional — like ordering a delivery or booking a taxi — neither question would be difficult. Survey capture (RQ1) would be trivial: the trip would have a clear purpose, a clear beneficiary, and a clear initiating agent. Just add a trip purpose code. Economic valuation (RQ2) would be equally straightforward: compare the trip to its market equivalent (¥800 taxi to the school = ¥800 of household-produced transport).
The fact that both questions are genuinely hard, and that PUDO has been invisible in surveys and accounts for decades despite being a huge share of household activity, tells us something specific about PUDO that resists easy measurement and valuation. RQ3 is the investigation of that “something”.


Why RQ3 makes RQ1 hard
The survey design problem is not technical. The problem is that for whom was this trip done? is a question PUDO providers themselves struggle to answer cleanly — not because they don’t know but because the motivational structure of the trip is relational, not transactional. Ask a parent driving a child to school whether the trip was for them or for the child and the likely answer is “well, both — it’s just what we do.” The provider role and the family role are fused; there is no mental ledger that says “this trip: 100% for the child.” That fusion is precisely what makes the survey hard. If PUDO were experienced transactionally (like a taxi ride), the for whom question would be obvious. It is because PUDO is embedded in care and obligation (RQ3 territory) that the boundary is blurry and respondents struggle to report it cleanly. RQ3 is the explanation for why RQ1 is a research problem at all.


Why RQ3 makes RQ2 non-trivial
If PUDO were perfectly commodifiable — if hiring a driver were an equivalent substitute — then replacement cost would be the correct valuation method. But RQ3 tells us PUDO is not easily commodified: households do not outsource PUDO even when market substitutes exist and even when the time cost is significant. The value of the trip is partly constituted by the relational bond between driver and passenger. The parent is not just transporting the child — they are present with the child, exercising care, fulfilling an obligation that a stranger cannot fulfill. That relational value does not survive substitution. Market prices are therefore a systematically wrong proxy for the value of PUDO time. Replacement cost (taxi fare) captures only functional displacement and misses the relational value; opportunity cost (forgone wage) captures the cost to the provider but not what the trip produces for the relationship. Neither standard VoT method is adequate. RQ3 is the explanation for why RQ2 is hard.


The structural implication
If RQ3 explains why RQ1 and RQ2 are hard, then it is the motivating insight of the paper, not a third finding appended at the end. Placing it at the end creates a paradox: the reader spends Acts 1–3 watching the author struggle with measurement and valuation problems, and only in Act 4 learns why those problems exist. The paper explains the solution before fully explaining the problem.
Moving the relational observation to the introduction does not delete Act 4 — it reorders the logic so the paper reads as: we observe that PUDO trips resist commodification; we argue this resistance reflects a relational structure that also explains their invisibility in surveys and their resistance to simple economic valuation; our paper addresses those two consequences. Acts 2 and 3 then become responses to a clearly motivated problem rather than technically interesting exercises.
The practical test: if Acts 2 and 3 were removed, Act 4 would still make sense as a standalone argument. If Act 4 were removed, the reader would not understand why Acts 1–3 matter. That asymmetry indicates which act is foundational.

title: Rizqi Ramadhan
text:
Hi! My name is Rama and I am a PhD student under the supervision of Chikaraishi Sensei at the Urban and Transportation Planning Lab (UTPL) of Hiroshima University.
My research focuses on the travel behavior of households having dual caregiving responsibilities. I am passionate about understanding how to capture trips that derived from other person’s needs and how caregiving roles influence travel patterns.
I am excited to share my research progress and projects on this website, and I welcome any feedback or collaboration opportunities.
